data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Maritime trade network hyperconnectivity created systemic fragility where nodal failures (Ugarit, Cyprus) propagated faster than palace bureaucracies could localize shocks, while Egypt's riverine autarky provided topological isolation", "mechanism": "Network contagion via multiplex ties (gift-exchange, diplomatic marriage, bronze trade) exceeds Dunbar-limited trust thresholds; when copper/tin routes fail, recursive honor-debt defaults cascade. Egypt's Nile corridor is a self-sufficient scale-free network with internal redundancy.", "scope": {"covers": ["Ugarit", "Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Mesopotamia", "Central Anatolia post-Hittite", "Egyptian frontier zones"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of continued palace functioning at Knossos, Pylos, or Ugarit beyond 1150 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian records showing dependence on Anatolian or Aegean grain imports during 1200-1150 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B tablets indicating palace awareness of and successful mitigation against supply chain disruptions", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Treats 'Sea Peoples' as symptom/network-restructuring epiphenomenon rather than independent cause. Requires testing against quantitative SNA models of LBA Eastern Mediterranean exchange."}
